title: Life Insurance Calculator Canada
seo_title: Life Insurance Calculator Canada | How Much Do You Need? | PolicyScanner
seo_description: Use our free life insurance calculator for Canada. Find out exactly how much coverage you need based on your income, debts, and family situation. Instant results.

secondary_keywords: how much life insurance do i need canada
_meta.conversion_goal: Tool engagement — get users to use the calculator, then immediately flow to quote comparison (highest-converting funnel entry)
_meta.page_role: Tool page. Calculator is the hero. Content explains how to use it and what the result means. Keep copy lean — the tool does the heavy lifting.
_meta.writer_focus: Short, purposeful content. People come here to DO something, not read. Explain the two methods (DIME and income multiple), show the calculator, then immediately bridge to quotes. No fluff.

seo_keywords.density_rule: Use 'life insurance calculator' 2–3 times per 500 words. This is a short page — use it in H1, intro paragraph, and near the CTA.
action_keywords: find out how much you need
natural_variations: figure out how much coverage you need
what amount of life insurance is right for you
estimating your life insurance needs in Canada
seo_keywords.real_example_to_use: James, 37, Ottawa — income $95K, mortgage $480K, 2 kids under 8 — DIME method gave $1.4M, income multiplier gave $950K. He bought $1.2M 20-year term at $72/month.
seo_keywords.avoid: Do not overload this page with content. The calculator is the product. Keep written sections to 100–200 words each.

page_data.heading: Life Insurance Calculator: How Much Coverage Do You Actually Need?
page_data.subheading: Answer 5 quick questions and get a personalized estimate in under 60 seconds. Then compare quotes from 10+ Canadian insurers.

_writer_notes.focus: Short hero — tool is above the fold. Get users to the calculator immediately. Subheading focuses on speed (60 seconds) and what happens after (compare quotes).
key_points: No email required to use the calculator
Instant estimate based on your specific situation

page_data.formula: DIME: Debt (mortgage + other) + Income (annual x years) + Mortgage (already included) + Education ($50K per child) — Existing coverage

_writer_notes.focus: This is a dynamic UI component — no static written content. The calculator output should show: (1) your DIME estimate, (2) your income-multiplier estimate, (3) a recommendation range, (4) CTA to get quotes for that coverage amount.

page_data.heading: How Is This Calculated? Two Methods Explained

_writer_notes.focus: Explain the two main calculation methods briefly. DIME is the most complete. Income multiplier (10x) is the quickest rule of thumb.
key_points: DIME method: Debt + Income replacement + Mortgage + Education. Most comprehensive.
Income multiplier: annual income × 10 (common financial advisor rule of thumb)
Why they differ: DIME is tailored to your specific debts; 10x income is a quick estimate
Which to use: DIME is better if you have significant debts or a specific mortgage balance
Real example: James, $95K income, $480K mortgage, 2 kids → DIME = $1.4M, 10x = $950K. He chose $1.2M as a middle ground.
_writer_notes.structure: Two subsections: DIME + Income Multiplier. Keep each under 100 words. Simple and scannable.

_writer_notes.focus: Quick bullets on what people forget to include in their needs calculation.
key_points: Forgetting childcare costs after a parent's death (can be $2–4K/month in Ontario)
Underestimating income replacement years — many choose 5 years when 15 is more appropriate
Not subtracting existing group life insurance from employer (usually 1–2x salary, not portable)
Ignoring education costs — university in Canada averages $30–50K over 4 years per child
Assuming a spouse will immediately return to full-time work — recovery takes time

page_data.heading: Got Your Number? Now Compare Quotes in 2 Minutes
page_data.subheading: Once you know the coverage amount you need, PolicyScanner compares 10+ Canadian insurers to find the lowest rate for that exact coverage.
page_data.cta_button: Compare Quotes for My Coverage Amount

_writer_notes.focus: Tight bridge from calculator result to quote funnel. This is the highest-converting element on the page.
key_points: Pre-fill the quote form with the calculator result
Takes 2 minutes to get your quotes
Ontario-licensed advisor reviews and explains results
_writer_notes.callout_after: Link: 'Not sure how much coverage to get? Read our guide: How Much Life Insurance Do I Need in Canada?'

faqs.question: How accurate is a life insurance calculator?
faqs.answer: A calculator gives you a solid starting estimate — typically within 10–20% of what a licensed advisor would recommend. For a precise recommendation based on your full financial picture, speaking with a licensed advisor is always wise.
faqs._guidance: Honest about limitations. Position advisor consultation as the smart next step.

faqs.question: What is the DIME method for life insurance?
faqs.answer: DIME stands for Debt, Income, Mortgage, and Education. You add: all non-mortgage debts + years of income replacement needed × annual income + outstanding mortgage + estimated education costs for children. Then subtract existing coverage. The result is your coverage gap.
faqs._guidance: Clear formula explanation — most readers have never heard of DIME.

faqs.question: Is 10x income enough life insurance in Canada?
faqs.answer: The 10x income rule is a quick estimate. For many Canadians with a mortgage and young children, it may actually be too low. If you have a $600,000 mortgage and two children, 10x income may not cover all your debts AND replace income. Use the DIME method for a more accurate figure.
faqs._guidance: Challenge the common oversimplification — be specific.
